source_metadata.title_raw: Susan K. Garea, Esq. - I-19-157 - October 10, 2019 - Oakland

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Susan K. Garea, Esq. 
483 Ninth St. 
Oakland, CA 94607 
October 10, 2019 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-19-157 
Dear Ms. Garea: 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Coastside County Water District 
employees Sean Donovan and Todd Schmidt regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Political Reform Act ("Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of 
interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions, such as 
common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. We also do not address any restrictions on 
incompatible activities the District may impose, which are also outside of the Act. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not 
the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
Nothing in this letter should be construed as an evaluation of any conduct which may have 
already taken place, and any conclusions drawn apply only to prospective actions. (Regulation 
18329( c)( 4 )(A).) Because you have not requested advice regarding any particular decision before 
the District employees, we can only offer informal assistance. Informal assistance does not confer 
the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83 i 14; 
Regulation 18329( c )(3).) 
QUESTION 
Are Mr. Donovan and Mr. Schmidt making a governmental decision to use the Crystal 
Springs Pump Station ("CSPS") to maintain Coastside County Water District's required water flow 
if CSPS is used only in emergency situations? 
1 The Political Reform Act is c~ntained in Government.Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 ofthe California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. I-19-157 
Page No. 2 
To the extent that Mr. Donovan and Mr. Schmidt are merely following the District's 
directive in maintaining the required water flow as determined by the District, neither has a 
disqualifying conflict of interest prohibiting them from maintaining the flow because neither 
official is making, participating in making, or using their official positions to influence a 
governmental decision under Regulation 18704. However, we caution that all decisions must be 
analyzed individually, and either official may be prohibited from taking part in any specific 
decision implicating the Skyline Funeral Home if the official is provided discretion in making the 
decision or exercises discretion outside of the District's directive. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
Sean Donovan is the treatment plant supervisor for the Coastside County Water District. 
Todd Schmidt is a Senior Operator in the District's treatment division. Mr. Donovan and Mr. 
Schmidt are tasked with operating the Denniston Water Treatment Plant, which treats the local 
water source. When the Denniston Water Treatment Plant is shut down, the District must supply 
water by using one or a combination of its several alternative water sources - wells, Pilarcitos Lake, 
or, if the conditions so demand, the Crystal Springs Pump Station ("CSPS"). 
Because the CSPS operation involves additional power and operational costs for the District, 
Mr. Donovan and Mr. Schmidt maintain the District's required water flow under their employer's 
directive to seek water from the non-CSPS water sources first. Water is only to be derived from the 
CSPS in dire circumstances. Case in point, CSPS use would only be warranted in the face of 
malfunctioning equipment, a storm causing turbidity higher than the state allows the plant to treat, 
or the volume of source water becoming too low to operate the plant. 
Superiors and the employees generally have planning discussions of future water sourcing 
prior to sourcing. Following the actions taken, the District's water sourcing is reviewed daily by 
many people. As such, all water derived from CSPS by Mr. Donovan and Mr. Schmidt is overseen 
and systematically reviewed. If for some reason CSPS was used when it was unnecessary, their 
work violation would be detected and corrected. 
Skylawn Funeral Home purchases water for irrigation from the District and is the District's 
largest customer. Mr. Donovan and Mr. Schmidt also perform some water quality operations, 
monitoring and reporting work for Skylawn that is not related to their employment with the 
District. Duties performed for Skylawn include monitoring of system chlorine residuals and the 
treated tank level, collecting state-required water quality samples and sending them for analysis, 
operating the chlorination system for one potable water well, and reporting on all these activities. 
Each have received greater than $500 from Skylawn in the past 12 months. 
Sky lawn has several sources of water including its own wells. One source of water is a tank 
that pumps from CSPS. Thus, if Sky lawn uses the tank to pump water from CSPS and, if during the 
same Pacific Gas and Electric billing cycle the District does not pump water from CSPS, then 
Skylawn pays the electric bill for the pumping. In sum, Skylawn pays more for water supplied by 
the District when the District operates CSPS only to supply water to Skylawn. Hence, Skylawn's 
,. 


File No. 1-19-157 
Page No. 3 
utility costs could potentially be impacted if the District operates the pump that draws water from 
CSPS while Skylawn simultaneously pumps water from CSPS. 
Mr. Donovan and Mr. Schmidt do not personally benefit in any way from the fact that the 
District may or may not pump water from CSPS. Their payments from Skylawn are not impacted 
in any way by Skylawn's utility bill. Mr. Donovan and Mr. Schmidt do not determine which water 
sources Skylawn uses and are unaware which sources Skylawn uses at any given time. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official at any level of state or local government from 
making, participating in making, or in any way attempting to use his or her official position to 
influence a governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to know the official 
has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on one or more of the public official's interests. Section 87103 als_o sets forth the interests 
from which a conflict of interest may arise under the Act. They include, as pertinent to your facts, 
any source of income aggregating to five hundred dollars or more in value provided to the official 
within 12 months prior to the decision. The Commission defined "making," "participating in 
making," and "influencing" a governmental decision in Regulation 18704 as follows: 
• 
A public official makes a governmental decision if the official authorizes or directs 
any action, votes, appoints a person, obligates, or commit~ his or her agency to any 
course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her 
agency. (Regulation 18704(a).) 
• 
A public official participates in making a governmental decision if the official 
provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the purpose of affecting 
the decision without significant intervening substantive review. (Regulation 
18704(b).) 
• 
A public official uses his or her official position to influence a governmental 
decision ifhe or she: [c]ontacts or appears before any official in his or her agency or 
in an agency subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or her agency for the 
purpose of affecting a decision; or [ c ]ontacts or appears before any official in any 
other government agency for the purpose of affecting a decision, and the public 
official acts or purports to act within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her 
agency in making the contact.. (Regulation 18704(c).) 
Based on your facts, neither Mr. Donovan nor Mr. Schmidt are making, participating in 
making, or influencing a "decision" within the meaning of Section 87100. Mr. Donovan and 
Mr. Schmidt are simply carrying out the decision of the District to avoid the use of CSPS unless 
there is insufficient water from other sources. Mr. Donovan and Mr. Schmidt do not exercise 
discretion regarding the amount of water required or whether there is sufficient water from other 
sources. Only if all other available water sources are insufficient to meet demand, will Mr. Donovan 
and Mr. Schmidt use water from CSPS. Furthermore, in such a case the determination that other 
water sources are insufficient to meet demand is systematically overseen. If Mr. Schmidt or Mr. 
Donovan ever operate CSPS when it was unnecessary to do so they would be violating their work 
directives and subject to discipline. 


File No. 1-19-157 
Page No. 4 
If ym.i have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
RPOC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
f.:/:=--14~ 
Counsel, Legal Division

If ym.i have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
RPOC:aja

CONCLUSION: To the extent that Mr. Donovan and Mr. Schmidt are merely following the District's 
directive in maintaining the required water flow as determined by the District, neither has a 
disqualifying conflict of interest prohibiting them from maintaining the flow because neither 
official is making, participating in making, or using their official positions to influence a 
governmental decision under Regulation 18704. However, we caution that all decisions must be 
analyzed individually, and either official may be prohibited from taking part in any specific 
decision implicating the Skyline Funeral Home if the official is provided discretion in making the 
decision or exercises discretion outside of the District's directive.
